narrative_blocks.header: He grew Franklin's fund for sixty years
narrative_blocks.body: William Minot managed Boston trusts and wills for more than sixty years, among them the bequest Benjamin Franklin left the city. Under his hand the Franklin Fund swelled from four thousand dollars in 1804 to well over a hundred thousand by 1868.

narrative_blocks.header: His Beacon Street house survived a great fire
narrative_blocks.body: In 1817 Minot built a fashionable Beacon Street townhouse opposite the Common, with tall wooden columns in the High Regency manner of London. A fire gutted six neighboring buildings in 1824, but his stood, and he lived there sixty years.

narrative_blocks.header: He died at ninety, his grandfather bankrupt
narrative_blocks.body: William Minot died in 1873 at the age of ninety, leaving an estate worth some three hundred sixty thousand dollars. His grandfather Stephen, a founder of King's Chapel, had by contrast died bankrupt.

bio.bio_blurb: Boston attorney who managed the Franklin Fund

career.notes: Managed trusts and wills from 39 Court Street for over sixty years.

bio.bio_blurb: Boston attorney who managed the Franklin Fund

career.notes: Managed trusts and wills from 39 Court Street for over sixty years.

narrative_blocks.header: She painted the terror of Niagara Falls
narrative_blocks.body: In 1818 Louisa Davis Minot produced a striking pair of canvases of Niagara Falls, the only works known from her hand. A daughter of the Massachusetts solicitor-general Daniel Davis, she rendered the dizzying force of the gorge with surprising originality.

bio.bio_blurb: Painter of early Niagara Falls canvases

career.role: Attorney specializing in estates and trusts

orbit_connections.name: George Richards Minot (1885-1950) (X00745) — Nobel Prize in Medicine 1934
orbit_connections.relationship: George's father James Jackson Minot was the first cousin of Charles Sedgwick Minot (HD2334)
orbit_connections.notes: Their fathers were cousins through the Minot family of Boston. George shares the 1934 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for pernicious anemia treatment. Not a Hooker descendant — the Minot family connection runs through a different branch.

notable.notable_blurb: Harvard anatomist and pioneer of embryology

narrative_blocks.header: He taught anatomy at Harvard for decades
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Sedgwick Minot graduated from MIT in 1872, studied in Leipzig, Paris, and Würzburg, and taught comparative anatomy at Harvard Medical School from 1880 until his death. He pioneered the study of cellular aging and assembled one of the era's great embryological collections.

narrative_blocks.header: He helped found a psychical research society
narrative_blocks.body: A rigorous empiricist, Minot was a founding member of the American Society for Psychical Research but resigned over its unscientific drift. He publicly attacked the claims of Theosophy and so-called esoteric Buddhism.

narrative_blocks.header: He asked why living things must age
narrative_blocks.body: Minot turned the microscope on mortality itself, studying how cells slow and decline with age. His 1908 book, The Problem of Age, Growth, and Death, made him a pioneer of the science of senescence.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale and Oxford honored the Roxbury anatomist
narrative_blocks.body: Honorary degrees came from Yale, Toronto, St. Andrews, and Oxford, and he presided over the American Association for the Advancement of Science. From 1912 he taught as Harvard's exchange professor in Berlin and Jena.

orbit_connections.name: Theodore Roosevelt (X00744) — Harvard classmate and ornithology companion
orbit_connections.relationship: Harvard classmates, class of 1880; both interested in ornithology
orbit_connections.notes: Roosevelt and Minot shared a passion for ornithology at Harvard. Minot published "The Land Birds and Game Birds of New England" at 17. Roosevelt later wrote extensively on natural history.

notable.notable_blurb: Ornithologist; railroad executive; train-crash victim at thirty-one

cross_connections.display_label: Harvard classmate; co-published Adirondacks bird list 1877

narrative_blocks.header: Published authoritative bird guide as a Harvard freshman
narrative_blocks.body: His 'Land Birds and Game Birds of New England' (1877) appeared when he was a seventeen-year-old college freshman. The volume remained an authoritative reference for New England ornithology for the next two decades.

narrative_blocks.header: Theodore Roosevelt's closest Harvard ornithology friend
narrative_blocks.body: He and TR met as freshman classmates at Harvard in 1877, both already serious ornithologists. They spent vacations listing birds in the Adirondacks and co-authored 'The Summer Birds of the Adirondacks' that fall — Roosevelt's first published work.

narrative_blocks.header: Left Harvard for the railroads at twenty
narrative_blocks.body: He withdrew from Harvard as a sophomore and entered railroad management — first with the Boston & Albany, then rising through several lines to assistant treasurer of the Lehigh Valley Railroad. The choice marked him as the rare Harvard man who chose practical industry over the law.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed at thirty-one in the industry he served
narrative_blocks.body: On November 14, 1890 the Day Express on the Pennsylvania Railroad's main line collided head-on with a freight train near New Florence Pennsylvania, killing him and twenty-one others. Theodore Roosevelt, by then US Civil Service Commissioner, was devastated; the friendship had defined his college years.

notable.notable_blurb: Boston physician and Harvard's third Hersey Professor

narrative_blocks.header: He built 65 Marlborough as home and office
narrative_blocks.body: Dr. Francis Minot, a widowed Boston physician, built 65 Marlborough Street in 1876 as both his home and his medical office. His daughter Julia lived there with him and, after marrying Felix Rackemann in 1886, raised her young family there.

narrative_blocks.header: He joined the Massachusetts General staff in 1859
narrative_blocks.body: After Harvard College in 1841 and the medical school in 1844, Francis Minot opened a practice on Charles Street and joined the surgical staff of Massachusetts General Hospital in 1859. He served the hospital for four decades, until his death in 1899.

narrative_blocks.header: He held Harvard's third Hersey Professorship
narrative_blocks.body: Minot became the third Hersey Professor of the Theory and Practice of Physic, a chair the family held across generations of Boston medicine. Contemporaries remembered him as a leading physician and a brilliant intellect.

narrative_blocks.header: He died at his daughter's Milton home
narrative_blocks.body: In his final years Minot left the Back Bay for Milton, dying in 1899 at the home of his daughter, Mrs. Felix Rackemann. He was buried at Forest Hills, in the wooded hills his family had long summered near.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Built and practiced at this 1876 Back Bay house

cross_connections.display_label: Boston physician whose granddaughter married a Wigglesworth
Great-uncle of Nobel laureate George Richards Minot

career.notes: Practice at 144 Charles Street, later 65 Marlborough Street.
On staff from 1859 until his death.

career.role: Hersey Professor of the Theory and Practice of Physic

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Grew up here and raised her young family

notable.notable_blurb: Judge and founder of the Massachusetts Historical Society

narrative_blocks.header: He recorded the vote that ratified the Constitution
narrative_blocks.body: As secretary of the 1788 Massachusetts convention, George Richards Minot kept the record of the close vote, 187 to 168, that ratified the federal Constitution. He went on to serve as a probate judge and chief justice of the court of common pleas.

narrative_blocks.header: He founded the Massachusetts Historical Society
narrative_blocks.body: In 1791 Minot was among the ten incorporators of the Massachusetts Historical Society, said to have gathered in his Court Street law office. He also wrote an early history of Shays' Rebellion and continued Thomas Hutchinson's history of the province.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's father was scalped at Ticonderoga
narrative_blocks.body: Minot married Mary Speakman, whose father Captain Thomas Speakman was scalped alive after a 1757 ambush near Ticonderoga in the French and Indian War. The relief party later found the captain's severed head fixed upon a pole.
